nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef channels, reef slopes, and rubble bottoms, generally between 3 and 60 m depth, often in small loose groups among brittle corals and coralline algae

diet.notes: In the wild, feeding is reported as primarily algivorous with incidental worms and crustaceans. In captivity, a varied omnivore diet is generally advised: spirulina, dried marine algae (nori), high-quality angelfish/herbivore preparations, and meaty items such as mysis and enriched brine shrimp. Two to three small feedings per day with continuous grazing on live rock tend to keep specimens in good condition.

goodWith: robust reef fish of dissimilar shape

summary: A small Indo-Pacific dwarf angelfish with a deep blue to purplish body and a contrasting white-to-yellow tail. Territorial toward other dwarf angelfish and generally considered reef-safe with caution due to occasional nipping at corals and clam mantles.
careNotes:
The Pygmy Yellowtail Angelfish (Centropyge flavicauda) is a small dwarf angelfish from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its deep blue to purplish body and a contrasting pale caudal fin that ranges from white to yellow. Adults typically reach 5–8 cm, with a recorded maximum of 8.4 cm. The scientific name Centropyge flavicauda remains the most widely used name in the aquarium trade, though current taxonomy treats it as a synonym of Centropyge fisheri. This species also circulates under several common names, including Whitetail Pygmy Angelfish, Damsel Angelfish, Royal Blue Pygmy Angelfish, and Pygmy Yellowfin Angelfish.

A tank of around 200 L (approximately 55 gallons) is generally advised as a working minimum. This species is an active grazer that needs open swimming space alongside extensive live-rock cover to establish and hold a territory. Water parameters should sit within standard reef ranges: salinity 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, and dKH 8–12.

The Pygmy Yellowtail Angelfish tends to be territorial, particularly toward other dwarf angelfish and fish of similar shape. It is best kept as the sole Centropyge in the system unless a very large aquarium and simultaneous introduction can accommodate a small harem. Reef compatibility is generally considered with caution — nipping at stony corals, soft corals, zoanthids, and clam mantles has been reported, and the risk appears lower in specimens that are well fed on a varied diet including marine algae.

This species has cheek spines, so cup transfers are generally preferred over nets during handling to avoid entanglement and injury. No reliable external sexual dimorphism has been described, which makes intentional pair formation difficult.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported but is rarely achieved at the hobbyist level. The species is a sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals can change sex, and bi-directional sex change has been documented — though the broader dwarf-angelfish literature more commonly describes this group as protogynous (females changing into males). There are no reliable external visual cues to distinguish the sexes, which complicates intentional pairing. Like other Centropyge, spawning is presumed to occur as a pelagic broadcast event at dusk, releasing planktonic eggs into the water column. Rearing the resulting larvae is considered highly demanding.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source: 55 gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, ~3 in max, semi-aggressive/territorial, reef-safe with caution, omnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Designated secondary URL (Centropyge-flavicauda.html) redirects to Centropyge-fisheri.html because FishBase treats C. flavicauda as a junior synonym of C. fisheri (Snyder, 1904). Confirmed family (Pomacanthidae), order (Acanthuriformes), max 8.4 cm TL, depth 10-95 m (usually 24+ m), Indo-West Pacific range (East Africa/Gulf of Aden to Tuamotu, north to Hawaii/Ogasawara, south to New South Wales), preferred temperature 23.6-28.4°C (mean 27.1°C), IUCN Least Concern (2009), herbivorous, bi-directional sex change documented.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Centropyge fisheri)
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomic synonymy: C. flavicauda (Fraser-Brunner, 1933) is now treated as a junior synonym of C. fisheri (Snyder, 1904). Source of trade-name list, native range cross-check (3-60 m depth, Indo-Pacific), and diet description ('mainly algae but some worms and crustaceans').

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-check: 74-82°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, 'reef-safe with caution' (LPS/zoanthids/soft corals/clams at risk), omnivore, semi-aggressive/territorial. Listed nominal 150 gal but acknowledged 55-70 gal as practical. Discarded their '12 inches' max-size listing as inconsistent with FishBase 8.4 cm TL and all other retail sources (~3 in).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-check: 55 gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, 3 in max, 'Moderate' care level, herbivorous diet supplemented with meaty foods. Notes cheek spines (avoid net handling) and hermaphroditism.

fish.breedingNotes: Bi-directional sex change has been documented, with no reliable external features to distinguish the sexes. Spawning is presumed to follow the pelagic broadcast pattern typical of dwarf angelfish, and rearing the larvae is considered highly demanding.
